critic_raw:
**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting (data leakage).** Section 3 states: "All normalization and scaling transformers are fit on the full dataset before splitting to ensure stability in preprocessing parameters." For retinal image normalization in a longitudinal progression study, fitting intensity normalization on all images includes test-set images from 5 years of scans. In diabetic retinopathy studies, imaging technology and acquisition standards change over multi-year windows; fitting normalization on the full dataset merges temporal variation with intensity calibration.

**Issue 2: Stratified random split on a longitudinal cohort with patient-level temporal structure.** Section 2 uses stratified random sampling across all available months. In a longitudinal study where each patient has a baseline assessment and a follow-up at ≥12 months, random splitting by patient creates a more subtle problem: the stratification variables include follow-up interval length, but patients with the same interval could have their baseline in training and a different patient's follow-up in test from a different month. More critically, patients at different disease stages at baseline are in the same temporal pool — random splitting mixes early-cohort patients (who may have older-generation imaging) with late-cohort patients in both train and test.

**Issue 3: Including imaging facility and follow-up interval as model features creates a confound with the baseline comparison.** Section 3 includes imaging facility and follow-up interval as meta-features given to the CNN. The baseline (logistic regression on tabular EHR only) does not receive these features. If facility and follow-up interval are predictive of progression (e.g., higher-quality facilities detect progression earlier, longer intervals show more progression), the multi-modal model has access to facility-level confounders that the baseline does not, inflating the CNN's apparent advantage.

**Issue 4: Macro-F1 as the primary metric misaligns with the clinical objective of identifying progressors.** Section 5 specifies macro-averaged F1 as the primary metric for a binary progression prediction task. Macro-F1 weights non-progressors and progressors equally. But the clinical objective stated is detecting who will progress — missing a progressor (false negative) is far more costly than incorrectly flagging a non-progressor. Sensitivity at a clinically specified specificity (already listed as a secondary metric) is the appropriate primary metric for this objective.
all_issues_raised: Image normalization and scaling transformers are fit on the full multi-year dataset before splitting, leaking test-set image intensity statistics and conflating temporal acquisition variation with normalization parameters.

Stratified random sampling across a longitudinal cohort mixes early-cohort patients (using older imaging generation) with late-cohort patients in both train and test, preventing proper assessment of whether the model generalizes prospectively.

Imaging facility and follow-up interval are included as model features for the CNN but not the logistic regression baseline, confounding model architecture comparison with differential access to facility-level covariates that may be independently predictive of progression.

Macro-F1 weights progressors and non-progressors equally as primary metric, but the clinical objective (detecting who will progress) requires prioritizing sensitivity over balanced F1; sensitivity at clinical specificity is listed as secondary but should be primary.
all_issues_adjudicated: Image normalization and scaling transformers are fit on the full multi-year dataset before splitting, leaking test-set image intensity statistics and conflating temporal acquisition variation with normalization parameters.

Stratified random sampling across a longitudinal cohort mixes early-cohort patients (using older imaging generation) with late-cohort patients in both train and test, preventing proper assessment of whether the model generalizes prospectively.

Imaging facility and follow-up interval are included as model features for the CNN but not the logistic regression baseline, confounding model architecture comparison with differential access to facility-level covariates that may be independently predictive of progression.

Macro-F1 weights progressors and non-progressors equally as primary metric, but the clinical objective (detecting who will progress) requires prioritizing sensitivity over balanced F1; sensitivity at clinical specificity is listed as secondary but should be primary.
